quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "EM wave speed"?

options.text: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity
Total A (mass number), total Z (charge), total energy (including mc²), and total momentum are all conserved
c = fλ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum; all EM waves travel at the same speed regardless of frequency
1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹

quiz_questions.explanation: "EM wave speed" is defined as: c = fλ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum; all EM waves travel at the same speed regardless of frequency. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Visible spectrum"?

options.text: Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)
The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; natural processes are irreversible
Red (~700 nm) to violet (~400 nm); the narrow band detectable by human eyes; different wavelengths = different colors
Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows

quiz_questions.explanation: "Visible spectrum" is defined as: Red (~700 nm) to violet (~400 nm); the narrow band detectable by human eyes; different wavelengths = different colors. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Photon energy"?

options.text: The minimum energy needed to eject an electron from a material's surface; material-dependent; determines threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h
Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei
E = hf = hc/λ; h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant); higher frequency = higher energy per photon
Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT

quiz_questions.explanation: "Photon energy" is defined as: E = hf = hc/λ; h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant); higher frequency = higher energy per photon. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Wien's displacement law"?

options.text: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei
θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface
λ_max = b/T; b ≈ 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K; hotter objects emit peak radiation at shorter wavelengths (bluer light)
v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself

quiz_questions.explanation: "Wien's displacement law" is defined as: λ_max = b/T; b ≈ 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K; hotter objects emit peak radiation at shorter wavelengths (bluer light). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Stefan-Boltzmann law"?

options.text: P = εσAT⁴; total power radiated by a body; P ∝ T⁴ means doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold
η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H; the maximum possible efficiency for an engine operating between temperatures T_H and T_C (in Kelvin)
Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars
F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Stefan-Boltzmann law" is defined as: P = εσAT⁴; total power radiated by a body; P ∝ T⁴ means doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "c = fλ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum; all EM waves travel at the same speed regardless of frequency"?

options.text: Gas law special cases
Electric field
EM wave speed
Beta-minus decay

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "EM wave speed".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Red (~700 nm) to violet (~400 nm); the narrow band detectable by human eyes; different wavelengths = different colors"?

options.text: Sound intensity level
Photon
Visible spectrum
Index of refraction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Visible spectrum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E = hf = hc/λ; h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant); higher frequency = higher energy per photon"?

options.text: Magnetic flux
Chromatic aberration
Photon energy
Superconductivity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Photon energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "λ_max = b/T; b ≈ 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K; hotter objects emit peak radiation at shorter wavelengths (bluer light)"?

options.text: Second Law of Thermodynamics
Wien's displacement law
Rydberg formula
Photon absorption

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Wien's displacement law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P = εσAT⁴; total power radiated by a body; P ∝ T⁴ means doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold"?

options.text: Stefan-Boltzmann law
Entropy
Wave interference
Magnetic domains

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Stefan-Boltzmann law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electromagnetic Spectrum is accurate?

options.text: Energy and Mass Equivalence is summarized by Einstein's famous equation E = mc².
Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.
Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.
Electromagnetic Spectrum encompasses all electromagnetic radiation, from radio waves (λ ~ km) to gamma rays (λ ~ pm).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Spectrum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electromagnetic Spectrum?

options.text: Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
All EM waves travel at c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum and satisfy c = fλ.
Electric Potential relates to the potential energy per unit charge in an electric field.
Radioactive decay follows N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), with half-life t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Spectrum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electromagnetic Spectrum?

options.text: Radioactivity and Decay describes the spontaneous transformation of unstable nuclei.
Light as a Wave examines the wave nature of light in detail.
The spectrum includes (increasing frequency): radio, microwave, infrared, visible (red 700nm to violet 400nm), ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma ray.
Sign conventions must be applied consistently for accurate results.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Spectrum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electromagnetic Spectrum?

options.text: Magnetic Properties of Materials classifies materials by their response to external magnetic fields.
EM waves are produced by accelerating charges and carry energy: E = hf (photon energy).
Beta-plus decay (β⁺): proton → neutron + positron + neutrino (Z→Z-1).
Rutherford's gold foil experiment proved atoms have tiny, dense, positive nuclei.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Spectrum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electromagnetic Spectrum?

options.text: The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).
The law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection (θᵢ = θᵣ), measured from the normal.
Energy carried by waves is proportional to amplitude squared: E ∝ A².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Spectrum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Spectrum?

options.text: Microwave ovens: 2.45 GHz radiation excites water molecule rotations, efficiently heating food while being absorbed less by plastics and ceramics
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Photoelectric solar cells: photon energy (E = hf) must exceed the work function of the material to free electrons and generate current—the particle nature of light in action

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Spectrum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electromagnetic Spectrum?

options.text: Radon testing: radon-222 (α emitter, t₁/₂ = 3.8 days) seeps from soil into basements; second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking
Thermal imaging cameras detect infrared radiation emitted by objects: used by firefighters to see through smoke and by engineers to find heat leaks in buildings
Nuclear medicine: technetium-99m (a metastable nuclear isomer) is the most widely used medical radioisotope, emitting gamma rays for diagnostic imaging of organs
Camera lens design: modern camera lenses contain 10-20 elements to correct for aberrations, provide zoom capability, and maintain image sharpness across the frame

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Spectrum.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electromagnetic Spectrum applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle
Electric motors: current-carrying coils in a magnetic field experience torque (F = IL × B), converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy
Medical X-rays: high-energy photons penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by dense bone, creating shadow images for diagnosing fractures and other conditions
Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Spectrum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electromagnetic Spectrum?

options.text: Microwave ovens: 2.45 GHz radiation excites water molecule rotations, efficiently heating food while being absorbed less by plastics and ceramics
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Camera lens design: modern camera lenses contain 10-20 elements to correct for aberrations, provide zoom capability, and maintain image sharpness across the frame
Noise-canceling headphones use destructive interference: a microphone detects ambient sound, and speakers produce the inverted wave to cancel noise

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Spectrum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electromagnetic Spectrum in practice?

options.text: Thermal imaging cameras detect infrared radiation emitted by objects: used by firefighters to see through smoke and by engineers to find heat leaks in buildings
Ultrasound imaging: high-frequency sound waves reflect off internal structures; the time delay and intensity of reflected waves produce medical images of organs and fetuses
PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring
Radon testing: radon-222 (α emitter, t₁/₂ = 3.8 days) seeps from soil into basements; second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Spectrum.
